coredata.dc:title: Green/red dual emissive carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of acid red 18 in food
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Ruoyu
Hu, Yaoping
coredata.dc:description: Quantitative determination of hazardous synthetic colorants in food is quite essential for human health. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence method is developed for sensitive and selective detection of acid red 18 (AR18) using green/red dual emissive carbon dots (g/r-CDs) as probes. The as-synthesized g/r-CDs exhibit two emission peaks at 508 and 610 nm under excitation of a UV light. When AR18 is added, the green fluorescence of g/r-CDs at 508 nm is quenched dramatically due to the strong inner filter effect; in contrast, the red fluorescence at 610 nm decreases much more mildly. The intensity ratio of I610/I508 has a good linearity with the AR18 concentration over the range of 0.05–8.00 μM. A detection limit of 16 nM is obtained, much lower than the most values reported by former analytical methods. Based on the g/r-CD sensor, accurate determination of AR18 in drinks and candy is successfully realized, demonstrating the good practicability of the proposed method for AR18 monitoring in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence detection
Synthetic food colorants
Acid red 18
Green/red dual emissive carbon dots
Drinks and candy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of green/red dual emissive carbon dots (g/r-CDs)
Sensitive and selective detection of AR18

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ20E020005 ), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science ( SKLACLS2004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City ( 2021J075 ), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of synthesis of green/red dual emissive carbon dots and their application for ratiometric fluorescence detection of acid red 18.
(a) TEM image of g/r-CDs. Inset in (a): HRTEM image of g/r-CDs. (b) Raman, (c) FTIR, (d) XPS full scan, (e) C1s, and (f) N1s spectra of g/r-CDs.

(a) UV-Vis absorption and PL (under 365 nm excitation) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of g/r-CDs. Insets in (a): photographs of the aqueous dispersion of g/r-CDs under sunlight and UV light (365 nm). (b) PL spectra of the aqueous dispersion of g/r-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of AR18 and PL spectrum of aqueous g/r-CD dispersion under excitation of 365 nm. (b) PL spectra of g/r-CDs before and after addition of 20 μM AR18. (c) The solid lines show the UV-Vis absorption spectra of g/r-CDs, AR18, and their mixture; the dotted line represents the physical addition of the spectra of g/r-CDs and AR18. (d) PL decay profiles of the green emission in g/r-CDs with and without AR18.

(a) PL spectra of g/r-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of AR18 (from top to bottom: 0.00, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50, 0.70, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, 6.00, 7.00, 8.00, 10.00, 12.00, 14.00, and 16.00 μM). Inset in (a): photographs of the mixture of g/r-CDs and AR18 under a UV lamp (from left to right, the concentrations of AR18 are 0.00, 2.00, 4.00, 8.00, 12.00, and 16.00 μM). (b) A linear plot of I610/I508 versus the concentration of AR18 in the range of 0.05–8.00 μM. (c) and (d) Selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor against common metal ions (1000 μM), biomolecules (80 μM), and food additives (80 μM). The concentrations of AR18 and EDTA are 8.00 and 1000 μM, respectively.
The results for detection of AR18 in drinks and candy using the ratiometric fluorescence method and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Green/red dual emissive carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of acid red 18 in food

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantitative determination of hazardous synthetic colorants in food is quite essential for human health. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence method is developed for sensitive and selective detection of acid red 18 (AR18) using green/red dual emissive carbon dots (g/r-CDs) as probes. The as-synthesized g/r-CDs exhibit two emission peaks at 508 and 610 nm under excitation of a UV light. When AR18 is added, the green fluorescence of g/r-CDs at 508 nm is quenched dramatically due to the strong inner filter effect; in contrast, the red fluorescence at 610 nm decreases much more mildly. The intensity ratio of I610/I508 has a good linearity with the AR18 concentration over the range of 0.05–8.00 μM. A detection limit of 16 nM is obtained, much lower than the most values reported by former analytical methods. Based on the g/r-CD sensor, accurate determination of AR18 in drinks and candy is successfully realized, demonstrating the good practicability of the proposed method for AR18 monitoring in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colorants are widely used as additives in the food processing industries to make products more attractive, appealing, appetizing, and informative. Natural food colorants, mostly extracted from plant tissues, are expensive and labile to heat, light, and oxygen [1]. In contrast, synthetic food colorants have lower prices and higher stability, possessing better performance in coloring, which have been extensively applied to substitute the natural ones [2]. Unfortunately, some synthetic colorants are potentially harmful and may cause cancer when consumed in excess [3].

Acid red 18 (AR18), also known as new coccine, is a common kind of synthetic dyes added in candies, cakes, and drinks [4]. AR18 contains toxic azo group and aromatic rings, therefore posing risks to human health. The maximum allowable daily intake of AR18 for humans is 4 mg kg–1 body weight [5]. In China, the use level of AR18 in foods should be strictly controlled below 0.5 g kg–1 (GB 2760-2014). However, excessive addition of AR18 is of frequent occurrence. Hence, it is very essential to quantitatively detect AR18 for food safety. Over the past two decades, several techniques have been developed for detection of AR18, including high-performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC/MS) [6], ion-pair liquid chromatography [7], capillary electrophoresis [8,9], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [2]. Nevertheless, some of these methods have drawbacks, such as complicated operation, long measurement time, and/or high cost. To date, it remains a great challenge to establish a reliable method for simple, rapid, and cost-effective determination of AR18 in food.

Carbon dots (CDs) with characteristic sizes of less than 10 nm have inspired vast interest because of their tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, good water dispersibility, inexpensive production, and easy surface modification [10,11]. These merits promote the utilization of CDs in various fields, especially in fluorescence analysis [12–16]. Lots of work has reported the applications of CDs as fluorescent sensors for detection of inorganic ions and organic molecules. However, in most cases, CDs are used as a single emission probe that is subject to interference from instruments and external environment. Few attentions have been paid to the ratiometric fluorescence technology based on CDs, which can overcome the above shortcomings by offering intrinsic self-calibration for signals [17,18].

In this work, we fabricate green/red dual emissive carbon dots (g/r-CDs) by a one-pot hydrothermal method ( Fig. 1). The as-synthesized g/r-CDs exhibit two emission peaks centered at 508 and 610 nm under excitation of 365 nm, with an absolute PL quantum yield of 12.7%. It is found that the green fluorescence of g/r-CDs at 508 nm can be remarkably quenched by AR18 through a strong inner filter effect (IFE), while the red fluorescence at 610 nm does not seriously decrease. Due to the ratiometric change of the two signals (Intensity610/Intensity508), a fluorescence sensor based on g/r-CDs toward AR18 is successfully built. The limit of detection (LOD) is measured to be as low as 16 nM. For practical applications, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor is employed to determinate AR18 in drinks and candy with high precision and satisfactory accuracy.

p-Aminoazobenzene, p-phenylenediamine, acid red 18, sodium benzoate, sunset yellow, and tartrazine were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Glucose, fructose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, Vitamin B1, and citric acid were received from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of green/red dual emissive carbon dots (g/r-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: g/r-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using p-aminoazobenzene and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. Briefly, 12 mg of p-aminoazobenzene and 20 mg of p-phenylenediamine were dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 50-mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After reaction, a dark red suspension containing g/r-CDs was yielded. The product was purified by passing through a 0.22-μm filter and dialysis (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) against deionized water for one day. Finally, the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation, and the solid g/r-CDs were got.

In a typical run, 20 mg of g/r-CDs were dispersed in 200 mL of deionized water. Then, 300 μL of the above g/r-CD dispersion was mixed with 2700 μL of aqueous AR18 solution. The final concentrations of AR18 were controlled in the range of 0–20 μM. After shaking for 30 s, the mixture was transferred to a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell for fluorescence measurement under excitation of 365 nm. The intensity ratio of I610/I508 was recorded for quantitative evaluation. In the selectivity experiments, AR18 (8 μM) was replaced by others chemicals at certain concentrations (1000 μM for the common metal ions, 80 μM for the representative biomolecules and food additives).

The candy and drinks including orange soda (Mirinda), carrot juice (Weichuan), and black tea (Master Kong) were purchased from the local supermarket. At first, 2 g of candy was crushed and dissolved in 5 mL of hot deionized water, and orange soda was sonicated for 30 min to remove CO2. Then, the solution of the candy and three different drinks were filtered through the 0.22-μm membrane and diluted 10-fold with deionized water. After that, 2400 μL of the pretreated sample, 300 μL of the standard AR18 solution, and 300 μL of the g/r-CD dispersion were mixed for fluorescence measurement. All the analytical experiments were repeated 3 times.

As seen in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2a, the as-prepared g/r-CDs are well dispersed and almost uniform in size with diameters of approximately 2–4 nm (Fig. S1). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of g/r-CDs (inset of Fig. 2a and Fig. S2) is low-contrast and no apparent graphene lattice is noticed in the particle, revealing the amorphous nature of the nanomaterial. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows a disorder D band at 1373 cm–1 and a graphitic G band at 1539 cm–1 with an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.65. This value is higher than that of crystalline carbon quantum dots [19,20], indicating that there are many sp3 defect sites inside g/r-CDs, which renders the poor graphitization [21]. The Raman result is in good accordance with the HRTEM analysis. The XRD pattern (Fig. S3) displays a broad diffraction peak at 2θ angle of about 23.0° corresponding to a lattice spacing of 0.39 nm, much larger than the (002) spacing of graphite (2θ = 26.5°, 0.34 nm), which further validates the non-crystalline structure of g/r-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are carried out to identify the chemical constituents and functional groups of the product. Fig. 2c displays several characteristic bands of g/r-CDs, including stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H at 3429 and 3220 cm–1, stretching and bending vibrations of Ar–H at 3122 and 833 cm–1, aromatic CC/CN bonds in the region of 1560–1650 cm–1, N–H and C–N bending vibrations at 1515 and 1401 cm–1, and C–O bond at 1123 and 1172 cm–1. In the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2d), predominant peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s are presented. The atomic concentrations of C, N, and O are 78.37%, 14.40%, and 7.23%, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be fitted into four components located at 284.5, 285.4, 286.6, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CN/CO bonds, respectively [22]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows the existence of pyridinic N at 398.6 eV, amine N at 399.8 eV, and quaternary N at 400.9 eV [23]. Taking all the data together, it is concluded that g/r-CDs are composed of sp2 N-heterocyclic rings and sp3 carbon defects, and bear hydrophilic groups such as –NH2 and –OH on the surface.

Fig. 3a shows the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous g/r-CD dispersion, in which two sharp peaks at 233 and 279 nm are assigned to the π–π * electronic transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n–π * electronic transition of oxygenic functional groups, respectively [24]. In addition, weak and broad shoulders at about 365 and 497 nm may be associated with the low-energy surface state [25]. Under 365 nm excitation, the aqueous g/r-CD dispersion emits bright orange light (Fig. 3a, inset). The corresponding PL spectrum shows that the orange light is not monochromatic. Instead, it is composed of two colors and can be adequately fitted by a double-Gaussian function, displaying a green fluorescence at about 508 nm (referred to band-I) and a red fluorescence at about 610 nm (referred to band-II). The PL spectra of g/r-CDs at different excitation wavelength (λex) are depicted in Fig. 3b. It is seen that the band-I emission highly depends on λex. Its peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength with decrease of intensity when λex goes from 360 to 440 nm. On the contrary, the band-II emission is λex-independent. Its peak position remains nearly unchanged under varied λex. We supposed that the λex-dependent behavior of band-I emission is resulted from the difference of particle sizes and diversity of surface emissive sites [26], while the λex-independent feature of band-II emission has a close relationship with the molecular fluorophores attached on g/r-CDs [27]. To further investigate the origin of the two emissions, the time-resolved fluorescence measurements are carried out. As shown in Fig. S4, the PL decay curve of the green emission (band-I) can be classified into two components with a shorter-lived value of 3.57 ns and a longer-lived value of 12.75 ns, which correspond to the recombination processes in the core and surface states, respectively [28]. Differently, the PL decay curve of the red emission (band-II) is fitted to a single-exponential function, giving a lifetime of 1.66 ns and reflecting the nature of molecular fluorophores [29]. Using an integrating sphere, the absolute PL quantum yield of g/r-CDs under 365 nm excitation is determined to be 12.7%, high enough to meet the requirement of fluorometric analysis. It should be mentioned that the ratio of the two precursors influences the emission peaks of the resultant g/r-CDs. As shown in Fig. S5, the increase of the mass ratio of p-aminoazobenzene/p-phenylenediamine results in the decrease of the intensity ratio of green/red emission.

The unique dual-emissive character of g/r-CDs inspires us to explore a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to detect the synthetic food colorant AR18 (AR18 does not emit fluorescence under excitation of 365 nm as shown in Fig. S6). As illustrated in Fig. 4a, AR18 has a main absorbance band at 507 nm, which precisely overlaps with the green fluorescence of g/r-CDs, indicating that AR18 can significantly absorb this light via the strong inner filter effect (IFE) [30]. In contrast, the spectral overlap between the absorption of AR18 and the red fluorescence of g/r-CDs is rather small, suggesting the weak IFE. Therefore, in the presence of AR18, the green fluorescence of g/r-CDs is decreased to a much greater extent than the red one (Fig. 4b). Based on the change of red/green signal (Intensity610/Intensity508), the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of AR18 is realized. To further confirm the IFE principle, several experiments are conducted. The absorption spectrum of the mixture of g/r-CDs and AR18 is presented in Fig. 4c. It is observed that this line (the black one) is simply the sum of the separate absorption of two individual components, which implies that there is no chemical interaction between g/r-CDs and AR18. Thus, no new compound is formed and the electron-transfer quenching mechanism can not occur [31]. Fig. 4d records the PL decay profiles of the green emission in g/r-CDs before and after addition of AR18. The two curves are nearly identical, giving the average PL lifetimes of 4.75 and 4.71 ns, respectively, meaning that IFE causes the fluorescence quenching, not Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), because the PL lifetime of the donor remains constant in IFE, but apparently declines during FRET [32]. Moreover, the ζ-potentials of g/r-CDs and AR18 are estimated to be + 16.4 and + 2.2 mV, respectively. Due to electrostatic repulsion, the distance between the donor and acceptor is large (>10 nm), so the possibility of FRET is ruled out [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of AR18
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve a better analytical performance, several detection parameters such as pH, the dosage of g/r-CDs, and the response time are investigated (Fig. S7–S9). Under the optimal conditions, a series of concentrations of AR18 are added into the aqueous dispersion of g/r-CDs. As seen in Fig. 5a, the total fluorescence of g/r-CDs gradually drops with the increased concentration of AR18, and the decline of the green emission is much greater than that of the red one. Correspondingly, the fluorescent color of the mixture regularly turns from orange to red (inset of Fig. 5a). Meanwhile, the ratio of Intensity610/Intensity508 (abbreviated as I610/I508) is in a positive linear correlation with the AR18 concentration in the range of 0.05–8.00 μM (Fig. 5b). The equation can be described as I610/I508 = 0.175 × C AR18 (μM) + 0.913 (R2 = 0.9991). The limit of detection at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 is evaluated to be 16 nM, much lower than the maximum allowance level of AR18 in drinks (0.05 g kg–1, equivalent to 82.7 μM) permitted by China National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment. We test the fluorescence response of g/r-CDs to common metal ions including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, and Al3+ at the high concentration of 1 mM, and find that only Cu2+ leads to the decrease of PL due to its good coordination ability with –NH2 groups on the surface of g/r-CDs, whereas others bring about negligible PL quenching (Fig. 5c). Fortunately, the interference of Cu2+ can be eliminated under the masking effect of EDTA. Fig. 5d shows that some representative biomolecules and food additives such us glucose, fructose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, Vitamin B1, citric acid, sodium benzoate, sunset yellow, and tartrazine also cause no obvious PL change. The above results confirm the good selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor toward AR18. In comparison with the former methods for AR18 detection (Table S1) [2,6–9] and other CD-based sensors for detection of synthetic food colorants (Table S2) [34–38], the proposed method using g/r-CDs for assay of AR18 is simple, quick, sensitive, selective, and inexpensive.

The practical applicability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor is assessed by monitoring AR18 in commercial drinks (orange soda [Mirinda], carrot juice [Weichuan], black tea [Master Kong]) and candy. After pretreatment as described in the experimental section, the real samples are mixed with the aqueous dispersion of g/r-CDs for PL measurement. Only a small amount of AR18 (in the safe level) is detected in the orange soda (2.73 μM) and the solution of candy (3.16 μM), and no analyte is found in the other two samples. We use standard addition technique to achieve a quantitation. As listed in Table 1, the recoveries of AR18 fall in 92.0–108.5%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) vary from 3.8% to 6.2%. These results are comparable to the data obtained from traditional HPLC, verifying the robust reliability of the proposed method for quantitative assay of AR18 in actual samples.

In summary, we have prepared dual emissive carbon dots simultaneously exhibiting green fluorescence at 508 nm and red fluorescence at 610 nm (that is, g/r-CDs) with an absolute PL quantum yield of 12.7%. Upon addition of AR18, the green emission of g/r-CDs is remarkably quenched due to strong IFE, but the decrease of the red emission is much less pronounced. According to the change of the two signals (I610/I508), the as-synthesized g/r-CDs possess an excellent ratiometric sensing property toward AR18 with a linear detection range of 0.05–8.00 μM and a low LOD of 16 nM. The g/r-CD based sensor also shows a satisfactory performance in quantitative assay of AR18 in drinks and candy. This work opens a new window to sensing applications of CDs in food safety.
Ruoyu Dai: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132420.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanorods as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy substrates for rapid and sensitive analysis of allura red and sunset yellow in beverages

Gao, Preparation of sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots: Applications in food dyes detection, solid-state luminescence and cell imaging, Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.281 (2022) 121581.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruoyu Dai is currently a college student majoring in chemistry at Ningbo University. Her research interests mainly focus on the optical applications of carbon dots.

Yaoping Hu is currently an associate professor at Ningbo University. He received his Ph.D. in chemistry from Nanjing University in 2015. After two-year postdoctoral experience in Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, he joined the School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering at Ningbo University. His research interests include the synthesis and applications of carbon nanomaterials.
